mappings.rationaleAI: Non-associative learning including habituation and sensitization (B.1), associative learning including classical and operant conditioning (B.2), and observational learning (B.4).
Abstraction, reasoning, and problem-solving as complex problem-solving capacities (B.5), including discrimination of relational and abstract stimuli (B.5.a) and insight and reasoning (B.5.c).
The full range of human behavioral responses including food getting, locomotion, and aggressive behaviour as basic behavioral activities (A) and the range of social behaviour (C.3).

Unjustified distinctions based on group membership relate tangentially to discriminations of relational and abstract stimuli in a cognitive sense (B.5.a), though the article's primary focus is social rather than perceptual.
Animal communication including verbal and non-verbal forms across species as a key dimension of group behaviour (C.2).
Language learning as a higher behavioral characteristic, specifically the capacity for structured grammar and vocabulary acquisition (B.5.d).
Ecological relationships including food webs relevant to food-getting behaviour (A.1) and habitat-related behaviour shaped by ecological niche (A.5).

Primate cognitive capacities including tool use and complex vocalizations as complex problem solving (B.5), with social structures ranging from pair bonds to multi-male groups illustrating range of social behaviour (C.3) and dynamics of social behaviour (C.4).
Play-based learning documented in mammals and birds as a form of perceptual and observational learning (B.4) supporting cognitive development and social learning.
Cognitive processes including reasoning, concept formation, and problem-solving as complex problem-solving capacities (B.5), generalized rule learning (B.5.b), and insight and reasoning (B.5.c).
Memory encoding, storage, and retrieval foundational to spatial learning including maze learning and navigation (B.3).
The mind as encompassing thought, perception, memory, and volition, relevant to complex problem solving and higher behavioral characteristics (B.5).
Friendship as a voluntary dyadic relationship involving mutual affection and reciprocity, relevant to the range of social behaviour among social animals (C.3).
Social units defined by shared characteristics, relevant to distinctions between social and nonsocial animal groups (C.1) and the range of social behaviour (C.3).
Emotions influencing aggressive behaviour and defensive threats (A.4) and the dynamics of social behaviour through affective states (C.4).
Cat food-getting through ambush predation (A.1) and avoidance behaviour including crepuscular activity patterns (A.3).
The cognitive capacity for drawing valid conclusions through logical processing, directly relevant to insight and reasoning in animals (B.5.c).
Toys supporting cognitive development through play-based learning, relevant to imitation and observational learning (B.4).
Organized physical competition as a structured form of locomotion and physical activity (A.2).
Childhood developmental stages involving progressive cognitive and social maturation through perceptual learning and imitation (B.4).
Psychology as the scientific study of mind and behaviour, broadly relevant to higher behavioral characteristics (B) and dynamics of social behaviour (C.4).
Adolescent cognitive gains and identity formation as perceptual learning and social development (B.4) and peer relationship intensification as social behaviour (C.3).
